name: more-itertools
what_it_does: Python library providing 100+ additional iterator utilities beyond Python's stdlib itertools. Includes chunked, windowed, batched, flatten, zip_broadcast, side_effect, seek_after, and many more. Essential for data processing pipelines, ETL scripts, and functional-style Python code. Docs-driven design with extensive usage examples.
use_cases: Process large iterables in fixed-size batches: more_itertools.chunked(iterable, n=100) for batch API calls
Create sliding windows over sequences: more_itertools.windowed(items, n=3) for time-series analysis
Flatten nested iterables of arbitrary depth: more_itertools.flatten(nested_list) without recursion
Interleave multiple iterators: more_itertools.roundrobin(*iterables) for fair scheduling
Split iterables based on predicates: more_itertools.split_when(iterable, pred) for grouping
not_for: NumPy array operations — more-itertools works with Python iterables, not NumPy arrays; use NumPy for numeric array processing
Parallel iteration — more-itertools is single-threaded; use concurrent.futures or asyncio for parallel processing
Streaming data with backpressure — more-itertools is synchronous; use async generators or reactive streams for async data
best_when: Processing Python iterables in data pipelines where stdlib itertools doesn't have the combinator you need.
avoid_when: Working with NumPy arrays or needing parallel/async iteration — use appropriate array or async libraries.

auth.notes: Pure Python library — no auth needed.

performance.rate_limits: None — local library. Generator-based — memory efficient for large iterables.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Standard Python exceptions (ValueError, StopIteration). Most functions are generators — errors surface during iteration, not at function call time.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Pure functional iteration utilities — stateless and deterministic given the same input.
known_agent_gotchas: Most more-itertools functions return generators (lazy) — the iterable is not consumed until iterated; call list() to materialize if needed
chunked() returns a list of lists — each chunk is a list, not a generator; for lazy chunking use chunked_even() or batched()
Python 3.12+ stdlib has itertools.batched() — for simple batching on Python 3.12+, consider avoiding the dependency
windowed() returns None for missing values at edges — filter or use fillvalue parameter to handle edge windows
side_effect() doesn't change the iterator but executes a function — its purpose is for debugging; don't use for critical logic in side effects
more-itertools has frequent additions — version pin your dependency to avoid behavior changes when upgrading

security_score_components.security_notes: Pure Python iteration library — no network access, no security concerns.
